claims.claim_text: The CBA 'Customizing Backstage' domain is weighted at 32% of the exam.
claims.span_hint: frontmatter: CBA Domain; section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The official CNCF CBA exam page lists domain weights and shows Customizing Backstage at 32%.

claims.claim_text: The Certified Backstage Associate (CBA) certification is officially offered by CNCF.
claims.rationale: The certification landing page is on cncf.io and identifies CBA as a CNCF certification.

claims.claim_text: Backstage was created by Spotify and is now hosted by CNCF as an Incubation-level project.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters; section: Did You Know? item 2
claims.rationale: The upstream Backstage repository README states that Backstage was created by Spotify and is hosted by CNCF at Incubation level.

claims.claim_text: The module's named 2024 financial-institution Backstage plugin breach anecdote is presented as a real incident with specific consequences.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Plugin That Broke Production
claims.anchor_text: In 2024, a major European financial institution experienced a severe, multi-day production incident tied directly to an improperly designed Backstage plugin. A platform team had built a custom frontend plugin that polled an internal Kubernetes cluster API directly from the browser to display real-time pod metrics. Because they did not route the request securely through a Backstage backend plugin, the frontend inadvertently exposed raw, long-lived cluster tokens to the client environment. A compromised third-party browser extension harvested these tokens, leading to an unauthorized lateral movement incident. The resulting breach cost the company millions in regulatory fines, mandatory security audits, and lost engineering velocity.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A misdesigned Backstage plugin can expose long-lived cluster credentials in the browser. If browser-exposed credentials are harvested, they can enable unauthorized access and trigger expensive incident response, remediation, and compliance work.
claims.rationale: This is a concrete breach narrative with year, sector, attack path, and impact, but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Backstage maintains a public plugin directory at backstage.io/plugins.
claims.rationale: The official plugin directory is real and directly relevant, but its primary host is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Backstage community-plugins repository exists and is licensed under Apache License 2.0.
claims.rationale: The official GitHub repository shows the community-plugins repo and its Apache-2.0 license.

claims.claim_text: Backstage has a monthly main release line and a weekly Tuesday `next` release line with fewer stability guarantees.
claims.rationale: The upstream versioning policy describes both release lines, their cadence, and the weaker guarantees for `next`.

claims.claim_text: Backstage supports exactly two adjacent even-numbered Node.js releases, and as of v1.46.0 those are Node.js 22 and 24.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 3; section: Hands-On Exercise Task 1
claims.rationale: The v1.46.0 release notes explicitly state that supported Node.js versions were updated to 22 and 24.

claims.claim_text: Backstage supports the last three TypeScript versions.
claims.rationale: The upstream versioning policy defines the TypeScript support window as the last three versions.

claims.claim_text: Backstage currently supports React 18, with React 19 still under evaluation.
claims.rationale: The official docs state React 18 support and ongoing evaluation of React 19, but the source host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The extrapolation that versions up to 1.51.x may have shipped by April 2026 is speculative rather than verified.
claims.anchor_text: Given the documented monthly release cadence, versions up to 1.51.x may have shipped by the time of writing (April 2026), but v1.49.0 remains our verified baseline.
claims.suggested_rewrite: v1.49.0 is the verified baseline referenced here; check the Backstage releases page for any newer versions before relying on release-specific behavior.
claims.rationale: This sentence mixes one verified fact with an uncited forward extrapolation about later releases.

claims.claim_text: As of v1.49.0, newly created Backstage apps use the New Frontend System by default, and `create-app` now uses `--legacy` instead of `--next` for the old system.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 4; section: Hands-On Exercise Task 1
claims.rationale: The v1.49.0 release notes explicitly describe the default switch to the New Frontend System and the `--legacy` flag.

claims.claim_text: The new frontend system creates plugins with `createFrontendPlugin` from `@backstage/frontend-plugin-api`.
claims.rationale: The official new-frontend plugin docs directly describe `createFrontendPlugin`, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Legacy frontend plugins use `createPlugin` from `@backstage/core-plugin-api`.
claims.rationale: The legacy API reference directly documents `createPlugin`, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Backend plugins are created with `createBackendPlugin`, and plugin-scoped HTTP routing is registered through `coreServices.httpRouter`.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 1 Key Differences table; section: Part 3.2 Backend Plugin Structure
claims.rationale: The backend-system docs describe both `createBackendPlugin` and plugin-scoped HTTP routing, but the source is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The new backend system reached stable 1.0 in Backstage v1.31.0, and Backstage encourages migration to it.
claims.rationale: The v1.31.0 release notes announce Backend System 1.0 and explicitly encourage migration.

claims.claim_text: A backend module may extend only one plugin and must be installed in the same backend instance as the plugin it extends.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 3.2 Backend Plugin Structure; section: Part 6.3 Register the custom action
claims.rationale: The official modules architecture docs say both of these constraints directly, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: In the new backend system, plugin-to-plugin calls use `auth`/`httpAuth` services and `auth.getPluginRequestToken` to obtain plugin tokens.
claims.rationale: The upstream auth documentation shows this exact request flow and example API usage.

claims.claim_text: The module states that Backstage's visual layer uniformly comes from MUI, which is now overstated.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4.1 Backstage's Relationship with MUI
claims.anchor_text: Backstage uses Material UI v5 (`@mui/material`) as its component library. Every visual element — buttons, cards, tables, dialogs — comes from MUI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Legacy Backstage frontend code commonly uses Material UI v5 (`@mui/material`), but current Backstage also ships Backstage UI components and is gradually moving some surfaces away from MUI-only primitives.
claims.rationale: By current releases, Backstage includes Backstage UI and no longer fits the blanket 'every visual element comes from MUI' wording.

claims.claim_text: Backstage supports custom themes via `createUnifiedTheme`.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 4.2 Custom Themes; section: Common Mistakes table
claims.rationale: The theming docs directly recommend `createUnifiedTheme`, but the primary docs host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Backstage plugin marketplace has 200+ community plugins.
claims.rationale: The official directory currently shows an active plugin count above 200, but the source host is off-allowlist and the number is live.

claims.claim_text: Backstage scaffolder includes built-in actions such as `fetch:template`, `fetch:plain`, `publish:github`, `publish:gitlab`, `publish:bitbucket`, `catalog:register`, `catalog:write`, and `debug:log`.
claims.rationale: The official built-in-actions docs cover these action families directly, but the source host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Custom scaffolder actions are created with `createTemplateAction` from `@backstage/plugin-scaffolder-node`.
claims.span_hint: section: Part 6.3 Writing a Custom Template Action
claims.rationale: The official custom-actions guide documents `createTemplateAction`, but the source host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Backstage auth providers expose sign-in resolvers such as `usernameMatchingUserEntityName`, `emailMatchingUserEntityProfileEmail`, and `emailLocalPartMatchingUserEntityName`.
claims.rationale: The official GitHub auth provider docs list these resolver names and behaviors, but the source host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Legacy Backstage frontend tests can use `renderInTestApp` from `@backstage/test-utils`.
claims.rationale: The legacy test-utils reference documents `renderInTestApp`, but the source host is off-allowlist.

further_reading.title: Backstage v1.49.0 Release Notes
further_reading.why_relevant: This release is the module's main version baseline and covers the New Frontend System default change.

further_reading.title: Backstage Release and Versioning Policy
further_reading.why_relevant: It defines the main and next release cadence plus Node.js and TypeScript support windows referenced throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Backstage Service-to-Service Auth
further_reading.why_relevant: It directly documents plugin-to-plugin token flows used in the module's backend auth section.

notes: Code blocks, quiz items, and most pedagogical analogies were intentionally excluded per instructions. Several framework-specific claims are real but currently land on official Backstage docs hosted at backstage.io, so they are marked needs_allowlist_expansion rather than forced to weak anchors.
